images.alt: Highly detailed photorealistic cinematic image of a student crouching in a snow-covered alleyway in a traditional historic Japanese town, gently petting a grey-and-white stray cat. Eye-level full-body portrait composition, shot on a 50mm lens with shallow depth of field. The student is in a low balanced crouch, facing slightly to the right, one hand gently resting on the cat’s head and the other arm relaxed across the knees. The expression is warm, natural, and candid. Outfit: classic sailor-style school uniform with a white long-sleeve top, deep teal collar with white stripes, red ribbon, matching pleated skirt, cozy pastel plaid scarf, cream knit socks, black penny loafers, and a small hair clip. Light snow rests on the scarf and hair. The cat stands beside the student on fresh snow, with grey markings, white chest and paws, and a calm, stoic alley-cat expression. Setting: narrow snowy back street with dark wooden facades, traditional architecture, utility boxes, soft falling snow, and a hazy winter background. Overcast daylight creates soft, even lighting with cool whites, greys, deep teal accents, and subtle warm scarf tones. 8k raw photo aesthetic, Sony A7IV, 50mm f/1.4 lens, f/2.0, soft winter color grading, cinematic realism, gentle emotional warmth, clean composition, no exaggerated features.

Cool-toned digital painting with grainy film texture, halftone effect, and rough painterly detail. A person sits on green rainforest grass, hugging their knees with a quiet, worried expression. Beside them, a small happy orange-and-white cat sits calmly. Lush foliage, large leaves, mist, soft rain, and a rippling stream fill the background. Vibrant greens and blues create a whimsical but melancholic mood. No line art, flat color, dramatic lighting, evocative composition, surreal emotional atmosphere.

external.description: The UK is launching one of the most aggressive social media crackdowns in history — a total ban on TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Facebook, and X for anyone under 16, set to take effect by spring 2027. Research links excessive teen social media use to anxiety, depression, and sleep disruption. Critics say enforcement will be nearly impossible, but supporters call it long overdue protection. The rest of the world is watching to see if this bold move actually works. Published 2026-06-18. Like, subscribe, and share your thoughts in the comments! record.text: Your 5-minute reality check: Open your AI tool right now. Type this: Write a sarcastic response to someone saying 'I love waking up at 5am.' Then rewrite the prompt as: Respond to 'I love waking up at 5am' with dry sarcasm, as if the speaker clearly does not enjoy it. Compare the two outputs […]

external.title: Original post on streetwi.se

record.bridgyOriginalText: Your 5-minute reality check: Open your AI tool right now. Type this: Write a sarcastic response to someone saying 'I love waking up at 5am.' Then rewrite the prompt as: Respond to 'I love waking up at 5am' with dry sarcasm, as if the speaker clearly does not enjoy it. Compare the two outputs. You'll see the second one lands closer to what you wanted. That's predictability in action. (4/5)

external.title: Original post on streetwi.se

record.text: And here's where people trip up. They think AI gets sarcasm and humor perfectly. It doesn't. Sarcasm relies on tone, context, and shared understanding. AI reads words, not your face. If you write Oh great, another Monday and ask AI to respond, it might cheerfully agree that Mondays are wonderful […]

external.title: Original post on streetwi.se

record.bridgyOriginalText: And here's where people trip up. They think AI gets sarcasm and humor perfectly. It doesn't. Sarcasm relies on tone, context, and shared understanding. AI reads words, not your face. If you write Oh great, another Monday and ask AI to respond, it might cheerfully agree that Mondays are wonderful. It's not broken. It just doesn't hear the eye roll. (3/5)

external.title: Original post on streetwi.se

record.text:
AWS Secrets Manager introduces safe secrets handling in the Agent Toolkit for AWS

AWS Secrets Manager now offers a secret safety skill as part of the aws-core plugin in the https://github.com/aws/agent-toolkit-for-aws, an open-source repository that equips AI coding agen...

#AWS #AwsSecretsManager

external.description:
AWS Secrets Manager now offers a secret safety skill as part of the aws-core plugin in the https://github.com/aws/agent-toolkit-for-aws, an open-source repository that equips AI coding agents with tools, knowledge, and guardrails for building on AWS. The skill lets developers use secrets within agentic workflows without ever exposing secret values to the underlying model or session logs.
 
 Until now, developers using AI coding agents could retrieve secrets as plain text without any guardrails, bringing sensitive values into agent context. With this skill, agents can securely retrieve and consume secrets without passing secret values through the context window, adding a layer of protection. To achieve this, the skill uses a two-layer approach. First, it steers the agent so the model never requests or receives a raw secret value—instead prompting the developer to clarify intent and constructing a command that uses the secret rather than retrieving it. Second, a child process resolves secret references to actual values only at execution time, outside the agent process. Together, these layers ensure plaintext secrets never appear in model context, session logs, or agent memory—without disrupting the developer's workflow.
 
 The secret safety skill is available today for all agent harnesses supported by the Agent Toolkit for AWS—including Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor—and in all AWS Regions where Secrets Manager is available. To get started, visit the https://github.com/aws/agent-toolkit-for-aws and install the aws-core plugin for your preferred coding agent. For details, refer to the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/retrieving-secrets-ai-agents.html.
external.title: AWS Secrets Manager introduces safe secrets handling in the Agent Toolkit for AWS

record.text:
Oracle Database@AWS now supports Oracle Autonomous AI Database Serverless

Oracle Database@AWS now supports Oracle Autonomous AI Database Serverless (ADB-S), a fully managed Oracle database service on Exadata infrastructure that automatically handles patching, tuning, and scaling. ADB-S is ...

#AWS

external.description:
Oracle Database@AWS now supports Oracle Autonomous AI Database Serverless (ADB-S), a fully managed Oracle database service on Exadata infrastructure that automatically handles patching, tuning, and scaling. ADB-S is available through both public and private offers on AWS Marketplace, with support for Bring Your Own License and License Included options.
 
 With ADB-S, you can provision an Oracle Autonomous AI Database directly from the AWS Management Console, AWS CLI, or AWS APIs without provisioning dedicated Exadata infrastructure or VM clusters. ADB-S supports four workload types - AI Transaction Processing, AI Lakehouse, AI JSON Database, and Oracle APEX - with compute and storage that scale independently based on workload demand. ADB-S includes Autonomous Data Guard for high availability and disaster recovery, automated backups to Amazon S3, and cross-Region disaster recovery. ADB-S integrates with AWS Key Management Service (KMS) for encryption, Amazon CloudWatch for monitoring, and Amazon EventBridge for event management.
 
 Oracle Autonomous AI Database Serverless on Oracle Database@AWS is available in the US East (N. Virginia) and US West (Oregon) AWS Regions. To learn more, visit https://aws.amazon.com/marketplace/featured-seller/oracle/ and the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/odb/latest/UserGuide/what-is-odb.html. To get started, subscribe through https://aws.amazon.com/marketplace/pp/prodview-qks5dl3hr7nfw?applicationId=AWSMPContessa&ref_=beagle&sr=0-1.
external.title: Oracle Database@AWS now supports Oracle Autonomous AI Database Serverless

record.text:
AWS Glue Data Catalog now supports business context and semantic search (Preview)

Today, AWS announces the preview of business context and semantic search for AWS Glue Data Catalog, helping you discover and understand data by semantic meaning. You can now enrich your Glue Data Catalog tabl...

#AWS

external.description:
Today, AWS announces the preview of business context and semantic search for AWS Glue Data Catalog, helping you discover and understand data by semantic meaning. You can now enrich your Glue Data Catalog tables, including those backed by S3 Tables, with glossary terms and custom metadata fields. You can also add skills to the catalog that direct agents to additional context about your data. With business context indexed alongside technical metadata, you can use the new Glue Search API to find data by semantic meaning, and ground your AI agents in trusted definitions rather than inferred context.

 You can use the new search capability to find tables in the catalog both by their structure, such as schema and table format, and by the business meaning you attach through glossary terms and descriptive metadata fields. This means an analyst exploring data or an agent reasoning about it can retrieve a table's definition, what its data represents, and how to use it correctly, in a single step. Any MCP-compatible agent, including Claude Code, Kiro, Cursor, and Codex, can get started with virtually no setup using the aws-data-analytics plugin from the Agent Toolkit for AWS.

 Business context and semantic search for AWS Glue Data Catalog is available in preview in the following AWS Regions: US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon), and Europe (Ireland). To learn more, visit the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/catalog-business-context-getting-started.html. To connect an AI agent to Glue Data Catalog, install the aws-data-analytics plugin from the https://github.com/aws/agent-toolkit-for-aws repository on GitHub.
external.title: AWS Glue Data Catalog now supports business context and semantic search (Preview)
